options: Frame both demonstrators explicitly as sandbox environments that simulate authenticated transactions without touching live services, with published security assumptions and scope boundaries that are reviewed by an external technical auditor before release.

Position the mobile demonstrator as a conformance test for a hypothetical future AltID implementation, focused on proving that FIDO2/WebAuthn on privacy-respecting Android can meet eIDAS Substantial-level assurance, independent of MitID's current architecture.

Release demonstrators under a restrictive research license requiring institutional access, with quarterly external review briefings for Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and relevant EU technical working groups, so the technical evidence reaches decision-makers without creating illusions of production readiness.
levers.review: The tension is between credibility (needing credible technical proof) and gatekeeping (risk of being weaponized to delay procurement change). The options focus on containment language but miss the question of whether the target audience — procurement officers accustomed to vendor-supplied products — will interpret 'feasibility proof' as permission to act or as license to wait for someone else to certify a production system.

levers.name: Procurement Language Insertion Strategy and Timing Lock
levers.consequences: Inserting specific platform-neutrality and fallback-access language into AltID procurement or related government digital-identity planning documents creates a binding requirement that incumbent operators must then satisfy or formally oppose. Early insertion (by month 14–16) increases the chance of incorporation before procurement drafts harden; late insertion risks being rejected as out-of-scope. However, overly prescriptive language may provoke stronger incumbent resistance or regulatory rejection if perceived as technically infeasible.
options: Draft three versions of platform-neutrality language — a mandatory requirement, a conditional requirement (triggered if supplier diversity assessment shows concentration risk above threshold), and a guidance note — and submit each through parallel institutional channels (Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, MitID operator, AltID program office) to maximize the probability that at least one pathway gains traction before procurement documents are finalized.

Anchor fallback-access requirements to existing regulatory instruments (eIDAS2 resilience obligations, NSIS continuity standards) rather than introducing new policy, then highlight the demonstrators and feasibility report as evidence that complying with those existing instruments requires platform-neutral design.

Embed platform-neutrality language into the AltID procurement evaluation criteria rather than hard requirements, so that bids including platform-neutral or fallback mechanisms score higher on resilience and supplier-diversity factors, creating competitive incentive without triggering formal rejection routes.
levers.review: The core tension is between binding the procurement (firm language = resistance) and merely influencing it (soft language = likely ignored). All three options assume that inserting language at any point will gain traction, but miss the scenario where procurement officers simply reject or deprioritize the language as 'research input' and proceed with incumbent-friendly processes.

levers.name: External Validation and Third-Party Authority Leverage
levers.consequences: Securing formal technical or regulatory review of the feasibility report and demonstrators from a recognized external body — such as the Danish Financial Supervisory Authority (FSA), the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA), or an independent security lab — strengthens the project's credibility and provides institutional cover for procurement officers who cite the review in support of platform-neutrality language. Conversely, a lukewarm or conditional external review weakens the signal and may be cited by opponents to justify inaction.
options: Commission a formal security assessment of the demonstrators and feasibility report from a recognized EU cybersecurity lab or ENISA-affiliated evaluator, published under the reviewer's name and available for public reference, so procurement officers can cite independent validation without political liability.

Engage the Danish Financial Supervisory Authority and the Payment Systems Council to issue a joint statement on supplier-concentration risk in digital identity infrastructure and the resilience value of platform-neutral fallback mechanisms, framing the issue as a systemic financial-sector dependency rather than a security opinion.

Establish an advisory committee of external experts from EU member states' digital identity programs, eIDAS working groups, and independent security research institutions that formally reviews the project's outputs quarterly and publishes a collective assessment affirming the technical and regulatory feasibility of platform-neutral authentication under eIDAS2 standards.
levers.review: The tension is between external credibility (which requires genuine independence) and project control (which requires predictability of the outcome). All three options assume that external validators will issue supportive findings, but miss the risk that an independent review could identify genuine technical limitations or regulatory blockers that weaken the project's case.

levers.name: Continuity-Planning Reframing and Resilience Governance
levers.consequences: Reframing the platform-neutrality argument from sovereignty or ideology to concrete continuity-of-service and catastrophic-failure scenarios — such as unplanned API changes, geopolitical sanctions, platform policy shifts, or regional outages — shifts the conversation from 'why change a working system' to 'what happens when the single-supplier assumption breaks.' This is a more defensible position in risk-averse government contexts. However, this reframing also implicitly accepts that current Apple/Google platforms are currently 'working' and risk-acceptable in normal conditions, which may undermine longer-term sovereignty arguments.
options: Conduct a published scenario analysis of three high-impact service-continuity failures (e.g., unplanned termination of MitID support on Android, API incompatibility during critical EU regulatory transition, geopolitical restriction on app-store access) and model the recovery time and citizen impact if no platform-neutral fallback exists, then propose fallback requirements as a mandatory continuity measure equivalent to disaster-recovery obligations in financial services.

Propose that Digitaliseringsstyrelsen adopt a formal supplier-concentration risk assessment framework for critical digital identity services, analogous to systemic-risk review in banking, and commit to platform-neutral fallback mechanisms as a standard mitigation for services where a single foreign supplier controls the primary access path.

Develop a continuity-planning workstream within the AltID program that explicitly models supplier-failure scenarios and demonstrates that platform-neutral or fallback-capable access paths reduce mean-time-to-recovery (MTTR) and citizen disruption compared to a single-platform-dependent model, then enshrine this analysis in AltID governance and procurement criteria.
levers.review: The core tension is between resonating with risk-averse institutional logic (continuity and resilience) and avoiding the appearance of exaggerating low-probability risks. The scenario-analysis approach may be dismissed as speculative if not grounded in concrete incidents or regulatory precedent from peer countries.

levers.name: Coalition Credibility and Sector-Neutral Advocacy
levers.consequences: Broadening the coalition beyond civil-society and privacy advocates to include financial-sector bodies, healthcare providers, public-sector CIOs, and continuity-planning experts shifts the perception from 'activist pressure' to 'institutional risk aggregation.' Public statements from these actors carry weight in procurement and policy circles. However, integrating diverse stakeholders also dilutes messaging focus and increases the risk that the coalition fractures if consensus breaks down or if incumbent operators successfully lobby individual members.
options: Establish a formal Danish Digital Resilience Council comprising representatives from the banking sector (Danish Bankers Association), healthcare (National Board of Health), public-sector IT (Danish Agency for Digitisation), academic security research, and civil society, with quarterly public statements on supplier-concentration risk and platform-neutrality recommendations that carry institutional weight independent of the research project.

Recruit 3–4 sympathetic municipal or regional public-sector CIOs to serve as public reference stakeholders, each prepared to testify to procurement officers or Digitaliseringsstyrelsen about the continuity-of-service and autonomy benefits of platform-neutral digital identity, thereby shifting the narrative from research-driven to practitioner-driven.

Engage the European Association of Digital Authorities (EUDA) and equivalent Nordic digital governance networks to co-author a position paper on supplier concentration in eIDAS2 implementations, positioning platform-neutrality language as a common Nordic and EU-wide good practice rather than a Danish-specific issue.
levers.review: The tension is between institutional credibility (multi-sector endorsement) and coalition cohesion (diverse stakeholders may have conflicting interests). Financial-sector actors may prioritize security assurance over sovereignty; healthcare may prioritize uptime over fallback complexity; incumbents may co-opt coalition members through selective engagement or funding.

levers.name: Technical Unburdening and Demonstrator Scope Defense
levers.consequences: Defining and defending a deliberately minimal scope for the two demonstrators — one mobile, one browser/hardware-token-based — reduces project risk, budget creep, and the danger of being drawn into productization cycles that the 24-month timeline cannot support. A lean scope also forces clarity about which technical assumptions are necessary for platform-neutrality and which are luxuries. However, a too-minimal scope may fail to convince skeptics that the approach is practically viable, and opponents may cite lack of real-world testing as proof that platform-neutral alternatives are 'still research problems.'
options: Freeze the demonstrator scope at authentication-only (no identity attribute issuance, no service integration beyond simulated public-sector endpoints) and publish a formal scope-exclusion document detailing five to ten features or integrations explicitly not included, with justification for each exclusion, to preempt scope-creep pressure and accusations of incompleteness.

Delegate demonstrator development to an external contractor with a fixed-price, fixed-scope contract and explicit approval gates tied to feasibility-report milestones, so the project team retains governance over scope but avoids the appearance of internal reluctance or technical bottleneck if the external team proposes simplifications.

Conduct a published comparative analysis of three existing open-source authentication projects (e.g., Keycloak, FusionAuth, or comparable systems) and justify why the project's demonstrators do not replicate those systems but instead focus on the specific constraints of Danish public-sector identity and eIDAS Substantial-level assurance, thereby positioning the work as targeted research rather than a general authentication platform.
levers.review: The tension is between disciplined scope (which protects the project timeline) and perceived incompleteness (which opens it to criticism as 'not real'). Opponents will likely claim that any unimplemented feature (e.g., multi-factor options, attribute issuance, service integration) proves that platform-neutral alternatives are not practically viable.

levers.name: EU-Level Standards Alignment and Nested Advocacy
levers.consequences: Engaging the European Digital Identity Wallet (EDIW) technical working groups and eIDAS2 implementation bodies to incorporate platform-neutrality language at the EU level creates a favorable regulatory environment for Danish procurement changes and shields the project from accusations of isolated national protectionism. EU-level standards carry weight in Danish regulatory processes. However, EU harmonization moves slowly, and platform-vendor lobbying at EU level is significant; EU language is often watered down into guidance rather than binding requirements.
options: Submit formal technical input to the eIDAS2 European Digital Identity Wallet Architecture Board recommending that certification frameworks include platform-neutrality requirements or resilience assessments, and coordinate with sympathetic EU member states (e.g., Netherlands, Germany, Finland) to co-sponsor the proposal, so it appears as EU consensus rather than Danish input.

Propose that the EU eIDAS technical working group adopt an interoperability testing profile for 'platform-agnostic' digital identity access (e.g., FIDO2/OpenID Connect without mandatory proprietary app stores), publish reference implementations, and include this profile in the eIDAS conformance-testing framework so that member states can reference EU standards when procuring digital identity systems.

Engage the relevant European standardization bodies (CEN/TC 224 and ISO/IEC JTC 1 working groups on identity and authentication) to develop a formal technical standard or guidance document on resilience and fallback-capability in digital identity systems, then reference this standard in Danish procurement language to anchor it in recognized EU/international technical governance.
levers.review: The tension is between enabling EU-level influence (requiring multi-stakeholder coordination and longer timelines) and actual procurement impact (EU standards are often guidance rather than mandatory). Platform vendors have established relationships with EU bodies and will resist binding platform-neutrality language.

levers.name: Demonstrator Authenticity and Supply-Chain Transparency
levers.consequences: Selecting between proprietary closed-source platforms and open-source stacks for demonstrators determines whether external reviewers can verify that the fallback model genuinely eliminates platform lock-in or merely relocates it. Open-source approaches invite community security review and establish technical credibility with procurement teams, but require more engineering effort to harden and document, and may expose architectural choices to incumbent criticism.
options: Build demonstrators entirely on open-source components (GrapheneOS, OpenID Connect libraries, FIDO2 reference implementations) with published source code and third-party security audit included in phase two deliverables.

Develop demonstrators using a mix of proprietary frameworks and open components, publishing only the integration layer and architectural diagrams while withholding implementation details to protect design decisions from incumbent counter-mapping.

Construct demonstrators as managed services running on a commercial cloud platform with closed APIs, prioritizing rapid feature demonstration and procurement-team usability over source transparency and long-term community ownership.
levers.review: The core tension is between demonstrator speed and institutional trust: open-source builds take longer but are auditable; proprietary approaches demo faster but invite claims that the fallback model is equally locked-in, just to a different vendor. Missed weakness: none of the options address whether demonstrators must support the exact same public-sector identity provider APIs that MitID uses, which means even an open-stack fallback may fail procurement adoption if integration points are not explicitly mapped.

levers.name: Resilience Messaging and Continuity-of-Service Framing
levers.consequences: Framing the platform-dependency issue as a resilience and continuity problem (versus a privacy or sovereignty problem) activates procurement and disaster-recovery stakeholders who are otherwise indifferent to ideological arguments. However, resilience language can be reinterpreted by incumbents as an argument for redundancy *within* the same platform duopoly rather than replacement of it, weakening the case for true platform-neutral fallbacks.
options: Position the project as a mandatory continuity planning exercise tied to critical infrastructure resilience standards, argue that a single supplier concentration violates implicit expectations of public-sector service continuity, and demand fallback-capable architecture as a regulatory baseline.

Reframe platform dependency as an operational risk during geopolitical disruptions, data-center outages, or service-level disputes, presenting the fallback model as insurance against supplier policy changes rather than as a sovereignty or privacy objective.

Advocate for resilience through supplier diversification within the existing duopoly—partner with both Apple and Google on redundant fallback mechanisms, framing the leverage point as contractual commitment to platform-agnostic API stability rather than architectural departure.
levers.review: Resilience framing gains traction with infrastructure stakeholders but is vulnerable to incumbent reinterpretation: operators can argue that redundancy across iOS and Android satisfies resilience without requiring platform-neutral alternatives. The missed weakness is that continuity risk is abstract until it manifests—the project lacks a concrete incident (outage, policy change, geopolitical event) that proves the vulnerability, so procurement teams may treat the risk as low-probability and defer action.

levers.name: Demonstrator Scope Defensibility and Feature Boundary Clarity
levers.consequences: Defining whether demonstrators must support the full citizen-facing feature set of MitID (biometric unlock, transaction signing, risk-scoring) or only proof-of-concept authentication determines both development cost and credibility with skeptics. Narrow scope is faster and clearer but invites dismissal as incomplete; full-feature scope is more convincing but risks project creep and delays phase-gate achievements.
options: Develop demonstrators supporting only username-password and FIDO2 passwordless authentication against a simulated identity provider, explicitly excluding biometric unlock, risk scoring, and transaction signing, with published scope boundaries in project documentation.

Build demonstrators that replicate core MitID workflows (including simulated risk assessment and limited transaction signing) to establish feature parity, accepting longer timelines and higher complexity to strengthen procurement-team confidence in functional equivalence.

Create two parallel demonstrators with explicitly separated scope: a minimal authentication-only prototype completed by month 12 to establish proof of concept, and a feature-richer variant by month 20 that addresses procurement teams' concerns about feature gaps.
levers.review: The tension is between credibility and timeline: narrow scope delivers faster technical evidence but invites procurement rejection on the grounds that fallbacks are feature-limited; feature-complete scope proves equivalence but risks missing phase-two gates. Missed weakness: demonstrators are not production systems, so feature completeness in a sandbox does not address operational issues like key management, user onboarding, support, and incident response that procurement teams care about far more than feature breadth.

levers.name: Procurement Language Insertion Vectors and Timing Lock
levers.consequences: The project must decide whether to push procurement language directly into AltID specifications or indirectly into broader EU standards and then reference those standards in Danish requirements. Direct insertion offers immediate Danish impact but may be blocked by operators; indirect routing through EU bodies is slower but creates external authority and legitimacy that Danish procurement teams cannot easily dismiss.
options: Target insertion of platform-neutrality and fallback-capability language directly into AltID design documents and procurement RFQs during months 18–22, leveraging phase-one coalition relationships and demonstrator credibility to influence Digitaliseringsstyrelsen procurement teams.

Pursue alignment with European Digital Identity Wallet standards and EU-level technical working groups, then cite emerging EU interoperability and resilience requirements as the rationale for Danish procurement language, creating external legitimacy that domestic operators cannot easily challenge.

Embed fallback and platform-neutrality requirements into statutory continuity-planning regulations and disaster-recovery frameworks rather than identity-specific procurement, forcing all digital service systems (not just identity) to address supplier dependency as a compliance obligation.
levers.review: The core tension is urgency versus leverage: direct insertion is time-bound to the AltID window but faces entrenched operator resistance; indirect EU routing builds legitimacy but risks missing the AltID procurement cycle entirely. Missed weakness: even if language is inserted, procurement teams will interpret vague terms like 'platform-neutral' narrowly or add escape clauses that allow incumbents to claim compliance with minimal change.

levers.name: Coalition Sustainability and Institutional Anchor Lock
levers.consequences: The project must choose whether to build a temporary issue-specific coalition (civil society, academics, privacy advocates) that dissolves after publication, or to establish a durable institutional body (formal authority, standing committee, research center) that persists beyond phase three and monitors compliance. Temporary coalitions are cheaper and faster but have no enforcement power; permanent institutional anchors require political capital and ongoing funding but create accountability mechanisms.
options: Establish a formal government-appointed digital-identity resilience advisory committee with rotating representation from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, privacy commissioners, and independent security researchers, chartered to review procurement decisions and audit platform-dependency risks annually.

Create an independent nonprofit research center or institute (potentially at Copenhagen University) dedicated to digital identity resilience, hosting the project team and future work, securing multi-year funding, and publishing annual digital-sovereignty assessments that inform policy debate.

Build a loose confederation of civil-society and academic stakeholders that co-publish findings, maintains a shared database of platform-dependency risks, and coordinates public advocacy without formal governance structure or permanent institutional home.
levers.review: The tension is between influence and sustainability: institutional anchors gain procurement weight but require political sponsorship that may compromise independence; temporary coalitions preserve autonomy but lack standing to challenge future procurement decisions. Missed weakness: even permanent institutional structures depend on political will and funding cycles—a change in government or digital-policy priorities can defund or marginalize the body, so institution-building alone does not guarantee lasting influence.

levers.name: Fallback Authentication Model and Technology Stack Selection
levers.consequences: Choosing between a hardware-token-based fallback (security keys, smart cards) and a browser-based fallback (WebAuthn over HTTPS without app dependency) determines accessibility, cost, and scalability. Hardware tokens offer stronger security guarantees but require citizen distribution infrastructure and training; browser-based models are more accessible but depend on browser vendors and may not be certified at the required assurance level.
options: Pursue a hardware-token-based fallback (FIDO2 security keys with PKI integration) that eliminates app and OS platform dependency entirely, accepting higher citizen adoption friction and requiring a national key distribution program as a long-term implementation commitment.

Develop a browser-based WebAuthn fallback that runs on any standards-compliant browser without requiring additional hardware or OS changes, prioritizing accessibility and rapid scaling but accepting residual dependency on browser vendors and OS integrity for push-notification fallbacks.

Design a hybrid model with two independent fallbacks: a browser-based option for routine authentication and a hardware-token option for high-risk transactions, allowing citizens to choose based on security preference while increasing implementation complexity and certification burden.
levers.review: The core tension is security guarantees versus citizen usability: hardware tokens are more isolated from platform vendors but require infrastructure investment that defers deployment; browser-based models are faster to deploy but depend on browser vendors, reintroducing platform concentration risk. Missed weakness: neither option addresses whether fallback authentication is mandatory for all citizens or optional only for those unable to use MitID—optional fallbacks will achieve low uptake and leave the core dependency intact.

levers.name: External Validation and Third-Party Authority Credibility
levers.consequences: Securing independent security audits, academic peer review, or certification from recognized bodies (e.g., European Cybersecurity Certification Authority) strengthens demonstrator credibility with procurement teams but requires significant budget, extends timelines, and may introduce certification bodies with incumbent relationships or conservative risk postures that slow approval. Relying on in-house and coalition review is faster but leaves the project vulnerable to dismissal as self-interested.
options: Commission a formal security audit from an independent third-party firm with prior Danish public-sector experience and eIDAS certification authority relationships, planning for review cycle time and incorporating findings into phase-three demonstrator updates.

Pursue academic peer review through published conference papers and journal submissions on platform-neutral authentication architecture, building credibility through community acceptance while accepting months-long publication timelines that may not align with procurement decision windows.

Establish a formal review panel including respected independent security experts, government cybersecurity officials, and MitID ecosystem stakeholders, meeting quarterly to assess demonstrator progress and issue signed validation reports that procurement teams can cite as evidence of technical soundness.
levers.review: The tension is credibility versus autonomy: formal external audits and certification bodies add weight with procurement teams but may introduce conflicts of interest or conservative requirements that delay deliverables; peer review builds academic credibility but takes too long to influence procurement timelines. Missed weakness: validation from any third party does not overcome the incumbent operator's ability to argue that their existing system is already certified and therefore lower-risk than adopting an untested alternative, regardless of technical equivalence.

levers.name: EU Standards Leverage and Nested Advocacy Escalation
levers.consequences: The project can invest in influencing European Digital Identity Wallet standards and NSIS working groups, using Danish advocacy as one voice in a broader EU coalition pressure campaign. This creates external legitimacy and multiplies influence but depends on finding allied member states and EU institutions with similar concerns, and requires navigating EU governance timelines that may not align with Danish AltID procurement windows.
options: Engage directly with EU standards-setting bodies and European Commission working groups on the digital identity wallet, co-author position papers on platform-neutral architecture and resilience requirements, and coordinate advocacy with other Nordic or privacy-focused member states to build collective pressure.

Focus advocacy efforts on EU-level interoperability and portability requirements (e.g., within the eIDAS Regulation revision framework), arguing that true cross-border digital identity requires that no single member state's citizens should be locked into foreign platforms, leveraging EU-wide digital sovereignty concerns.

Build a secondary coalition of European civil-society organizations and security researchers independent of government channels, create a grassroots advocacy campaign publicizing platform-dependency risks, and attempt to influence EU public discourse and political pressure on member state governments rather than working through formal standards bodies.
levers.review: The tension is leverage versus control: formal EU engagement builds external authority but depends on other member states' priorities and EU consensus processes that are slow; grassroots coalition-building is faster and avoids bureaucratic delays but has less formal authority over procurement. Missed weakness: even if EU standards language emerges, member states (including Denmark) retain discretion to implement or interpret loosely—the project lacks enforcement mechanisms to ensure that EU standards translate into actual Danish procurement requirements or technology choices.

levers.name: Contingency Exit Triggers and Phase-Gate Realism
levers.consequences: The project must define clear triggers for phase advancement or scope reduction if institutional engagement is weak, funding becomes uncertain, or incumbent operator resistance is unexpectedly high. Optimistic phase-gating risks squandering budget on phases that cannot succeed; overly conservative triggers risk abandoning the project prematurely. The choice determines how the project adapts to political and institutional realities that phase one may reveal.
options: Set conditional phase gates: phase two funding requires documented institutional engagement (written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or MitID operator) by month 6 and a published feasibility report by month 8; if either milestone is missed, phase two pivots to publication and EU-focused standards work rather than pursuing Danish procurement influence.

Commit to executing all three phases at full scope regardless of phase-one institutional outcomes, reasoning that technical demonstrators and standards work have value independent of Danish procurement adoption and that longer-term political conditions may shift in favor of platform-neutral requirements.

Establish a mid-project (month 12) comprehensive reassessment: pause all work, conduct stakeholder interviews with procurement teams and operators, and decide whether to continue toward full procurement insertion or to reframe phase three exclusively toward academic publication and EU standards contribution.
levers.review: The tension is opportunity cost versus realism: ambitious full-scope execution maximizes learning but risks budget waste if procurement pathways close; aggressive exit triggers preserve resources but may abandon leverage before institutions have had time to absorb findings. Missed weakness: institutional inertia means that rejection or silence in months 1–8 does not prove the issue is unimportant—procurement teams and regulators may simply be slow, not actively opposed, so timing-based exit triggers may be premature.

levers.name: Team Composition and Dependency Avoidance
levers.consequences: Building a team with single points of failure in mobile security, regulatory expertise, or policy coordination creates schedule and institutional risk if a key person departs or becomes unavailable. Distributing expertise and cross-training increases overhead and team coordination cost but reduces vulnerability to personnel loss and ensures that project continuity and institutional relationships are not hostage to individual availability.
options: Hire two independent mobile-security researchers with complementary expertise (e.g., one focused on authentication protocols, one on OS-level integrity models) and structure demonstrator work so both are familiar with core architectural decisions and can substitute for each other if one becomes unavailable.

Recruit a primary lead researcher and a single technical architect, supported by external contractors for specialized work (security review, hardware-token implementation, EU standards research), keeping core team lean and decision-making fast while accepting dependency on contractor availability for critical phases.

Establish formal cross-training protocols where every core team member (researcher, regulatory specialist, policy coordinator, technical lead) documents their domain knowledge in shared wikis and design documents, and holds monthly knowledge-transfer sessions to ensure no single person is irreplaceable.
levers.review: The tension is efficiency versus resilience: small lean teams make decisions faster and reduce overhead but create personnel risks that can derail the project; distributed expertise and cross-training protect continuity but add coordination cost and slow decision velocity. Missed weakness: documentation and cross-training are excellent for institutional knowledge but do not address relationship risk—stakeholder trust and political capital are often tied to individuals, so losing a key policy coordinator may damage institutional relationships that cannot easily be rebuilt.

levers.name: Geographic Expansion and Regulatory Arbitrage
levers.consequences: The project is currently scoped to Denmark but could expand to neighboring EU jurisdictions (Estonia, Netherlands, Sweden) that share similar digital-sovereignty concerns and have different regulatory or political leverage points. Expansion multiplies coalition power and standards influence but dilutes focus, strains budget, and introduces additional language and regulatory complexity. Staying Denmark-focused preserves clarity but reduces the external authority that comes from multi-country alignment.
options: Scope the project exclusively to Denmark, investing all advocacy effort in Digitaliseringsstyrelsen and Danish procurement processes, and limit EU engagement to publication and standards-setting activities without formal multi-country coordination or comparative policy work.

Establish formal partnerships with digital-identity research institutions or government agencies in Estonia, Netherlands, or Sweden, coordinate demonstrators and policy proposals across jurisdictions, and leverage multi-country advocacy to influence EU-level standards as a bloc of aligned member states.

Begin phase one with Denmark focus but explicitly plan for controlled geographic expansion in phases two and three: if Danish institutional engagement is weak, pivot toward building a Nordic or Baltic digital-identity resilience coalition that can coordinate on EU standards and procurement language.
levers.review: The core tension is focus versus leverage: Denmark-only work is clearer and more actionable but depends entirely on Danish political conditions; multi-country expansion creates external legitimacy and EU influence but requires coordination overhead and may diffuse advocacy messages. Missed weakness: even if multiple countries align on resilience requirements, harmonizing those requirements into actual procurement language is a lengthy negotiation—EU-level coordination does not guarantee faster implementation than bilateral Danish work.

levers.name: Publication Strategy and Narrative Timing
levers.consequences: Deciding when and how to publish findings determines whether the project shapes policy through advance influence (early publication seeding debate) or through validated authority (waiting for demonstrators and reviews to complete before publishing). Early publication risks incomplete evidence but may influence procurement timelines; late publication provides stronger evidence but may miss policy windows. The choice also determines whether publications are targeted at policy teams and procurement professionals or at academic audiences and civil society.
options: Publish preliminary findings and policy proposals early (months 8–10) through practitioner-focused outlets and government consultation channels, intentionally seeding debate and inviting procurement team feedback while work is still in progress, accepting weaker evidence quality in exchange for timeline influence.

Sequence publications defensively: publish feasibility findings after phase one, demonstrator results after phase two, and procurement recommendations only in phase three once all evidence is complete, prioritizing academic credibility and resilience against incumbent criticism over speed of policy influence.

Establish a dual-track publication strategy: release technical findings through academic journals and security conferences (slow but authoritative), while simultaneously feeding policy recommendations to government and procurement teams through direct engagement and summary documents (fast and actionable but less rigorous).
levers.review: The tension is speed versus evidence: early publication shapes timelines but invites dismissal as premature; late publication is more defensible but may miss procurement windows entirely. Missed weakness: publication strategy does not determine whether procurement teams will actually read and act on the findings—even authoritative, well-timed publications face institutional inertia and incumbent operator pressure that no publication strategy can overcome.

levers.name: Demonstrator Certification Path and Standards Compliance Ambition
levers.consequences: Choosing whether to pursue formal eIDAS or NSIS certification for demonstrators versus positioning them as non-certified research artifacts affects credibility with procurement teams and long-term viability as policy evidence. Full certification demonstrates production readiness but is expensive, time-consuming, and depends on certification authorities' willingness to review non-incumbent systems; non-certified research status is faster and cheaper but leaves procurement teams with a ready excuse that the alternatives are not properly vetted.
options: Pursue formal NSIS Substantial assurance level certification or equivalent eIDAS compliance for at least one demonstrator during phase three, engaging certification authorities early and budgeting for compliance overhead, accepting that certification timelines may extend beyond month 24 but providing procurement teams with formal validation.

Position demonstrators as non-certified research prototypes with published security review and threat modeling, explicitly acknowledging they are not production-ready, and instead argue that certification pathways *exist* for platform-neutral models and that incumbent operators have had years to build them—the issue is not technical feasibility but political choice.

Develop a hybrid approach: one demonstrator pursues formal NSIS certification (high effort, long timeline) while a second demonstrator remains non-certified research, allowing the project to offer both a certified proof point and a faster technical validation without betting the entire certification pathway on a single system.
levers.review: The tension is credibility versus timeline: pursuing formal certification makes demonstrators more impressive to procurement teams but risks missing phase-completion deadlines or revealing certification bodies' conservative requirements that undermine the fallback case; non-certified status is faster but invites dismissal. Missed weakness: certification of a research system does not guarantee that operators or procurement teams will view it as equivalent to a production-grade MitID—certification bodies may impose higher security or feature requirements for systems used at scale than for research prototypes.

levers.name: Incumbent Operator Engagement Strategy and Cooperation Realism
levers.consequences: The project must decide whether to treat the MitID operator as a potential collaborator (seeking their feedback and buy-in), a competitive threat (demonstrating alternatives without their input), or a hybrid (sharing findings but making independent recommendations). Collaboration risks co-option and watered-down conclusions; competition risks antagonizing a powerful incumbent with influence over regulators; hybrids are ambiguous and may satisfy neither side.
options: Engage the MitID operator as a formal project partner or advisory board member, seeking their technical input on integration points and demonstrator design, accepting that their feedback will be conservative but gaining institutional legitimacy and potentially neutralizing operator resistance to conclusions.

Treat the operator as a competitor and design the project explicitly to bypass their input: conduct independent security assessments of MitID without operator cooperation, build demonstrators without disclosing architectural choices to the operator, and publish recommendations without pre-clearing them with the incumbent.

Adopt a selective transparency approach: share non-sensitive technical architecture questions with the operator and invite their input on feasibility concerns, but conduct independent risk assessment and publish conclusions regardless of operator approval, treating their input as one perspective rather than a blocking entity.
levers.review: The core tension is institutional acceptance versus independence: including operators increases buy-in risk and potentially compromises findings, but excluding them antagonizes a stakeholder with regulatory relationships; selective engagement splits the difference but may alienate both sides. Missed weakness: regardless of engagement strategy, the operator can point to any platform-neutral alternative and argue that it lacks the maturity, regulatory history, and operational robustness of MitID, making a competitive comparison meaningless—the operator will claim the alternatives are not comparable until they are deployed at scale, which is post-procurement.

levers.name: Budget Allocation and Contingency Capacity Planning
levers.consequences: The project has a fixed 10.5 million DKK budget allocated across three phases with phase-specific funding gates. Allocating more to phase one (feasibility, coalition, policy work) strengthens institutional foundations and reduces phase-two uncertainty, but defers technical demonstrator investment; backloading toward phases two and three maximizes technical credibility but risks weak institutional positioning if phase-one policy engagement fails. The choice determines which project risks are absorbed versus deferred.
options: Front-load budget toward phase one (4 million DKK) for extended feasibility, stakeholder mapping, and coalition-building, allocating 3 million to phase two and 3.5 million to phase three, betting that stronger institutional positioning in phase one will reduce phase-two and phase-three risks.

Allocate budget evenly across phases (3.5 million each) to maintain balance between feasibility, demonstrators, and procurement work, accepting that institutional positioning in phase one may be weaker but preserving maximum resources for technical and advocacy work in later phases.

Back-load budget toward phases two and three (2.5 million phase one, 4 million phase two, 4 million phase three), maximizing technical demonstrator resources and procurement engagement at the expense of early-stage coalition and policy work, betting that credible technical evidence will overcome weak early institutional positioning.
levers.review: The tension is institutional risk versus technical leverage: front-loading protects against institutional rejection early on but wastes money on policy work that may be ineffective; back-loading maximizes technical credibility but risks discovering in month 8 that procurement pathways are closed. Missed weakness: budget allocation does not affect the fundamental constraint—even with optimal allocation, the project cannot purchase political will or overcome incumbent resistance if procurement decisions are already made or if digital-policy priorities shift due to government change.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "Switching from seasonal contract labor to year-round employees stabilizes harvest quality, but the idle-wage burden during the 5-month off-season adds a fixed cost that erases the per-unit savings unless utilization reaches year-round levels."
     - "Each additional clinical site requires its own IRB approval, site-initiation visit, and staff credentialing — a sequential overhead that compounds rather than parallelizes, so doubling site count does not halve enrollment time."
     - "Pooling catastrophe risk across three coastal regions reduces expected annual loss on paper, but a single regional hurricane season can correlate all three simultaneously, turning the diversification assumption into a concentration risk at the worst possible moment."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Length Limits**
   - Keep each `review_lever` under 2 sentences (aim for 20–40 words). Name the tension and the missed weakness concisely.
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Denmark's national digital identity infrastructure currently depends in practice on Apple iOS and Google Android for broad citizen access to essential services such as banking, tax filing, healthcare portals, and public administration. This dependency is not limited to app-store distribution. MitID's anti-fraud and app-integrity model relies on platform-controlled signals concerning approved installation channels and device integrity, which means that critical parts of the trust model for Danish digital identity are mediated by two foreign commercial platform providers. Even where direct observation of specific authentication events is not publicly documented, the current setup leaves Danish digital identity structurally dependent on platform vendors that control app availability, device trust signals, and surrounding telemetry environments. This creates not only a digital-sovereignty problem, but also a resilience and contingency problem: essential public access depends on a narrow supplier base and lacks a clearly platform-neutral fallback path.

This matters because Denmark's public-sector digital strategy for 2026–2029 explicitly calls for reduced dependence on a small number of large foreign technology suppliers and for stronger digital sovereignty. The issue should therefore not be framed only as privacy or ideology, but as a concrete public-sector risk involving supplier concentration, reduced strategic autonomy, continuity-of-service exposure, and the absence of a robust fallback authentication path if platform policies, integrity APIs, distribution terms, or geopolitical conditions change.

This project does not attempt to build and certify a production-ready replacement for MitID within the current trust framework. That would be unrealistic. The MitID ecosystem is conservative, certification-heavy, and tightly coupled to Apple/Google platform integrity signals. Instead, the project's objective is to establish the technical, regulatory, and political basis for formal platform-neutral access requirements and a certified fallback authentication path for Danish digital identity. The primary intervention point is not retrofitting MitID itself, but influencing AltID — Denmark's staged eIDAS2 wallet rollout — together with related procurement, standards, and continuity-planning decisions before the next identity stack hardens the same dependencies.

The project is structured in three phases over 24 months with a total budget of 10.5 million DKK, gated across phases (3.0 million for phase one, 4.0 million for phase two, 3.5 million for phase three). The higher budget reflects the need for credible technical work, policy engagement, external review, institutional overhead, and contingency capacity. Do not assume that 8 million DKK is sufficient for the same scope.

Phase one (months 1–8) is a feasibility, risk, and coalition-building phase. The technical work maps the relevant MitID dependency surface, especially platform integrity assumptions, app-distribution constraints, and the architectural conditions required for a platform-neutral or fallback trust model under eIDAS and NSIS at level "Substantial" or higher. The policy work reframes the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, procurement neutrality, and supplier concentration risk, while also addressing privacy and democratic accountability. In parallel, the advocacy work builds a coalition spanning civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee. The deliverables are: (1) a published feasibility and risk report, (2) a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, (3) a fallback-authentication concept note oriented toward AltID and future procurement requirements, and (4) a permit/approval and stakeholder matrix with lead times, decision gates, and identified blockers. Phase one funding is unconditional.

Phase two (months 9–16) develops non-production technical demonstrators. These are not certified MitID clients and do not attempt production integration with live MitID systems. The main demonstrator authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards such as FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or one comparable privacy-respecting Android-compatible platform. A secondary demonstrator explores a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether. The goal is not to prove immediate deployability, but to produce credible technical evidence that secure, standards-based, fallback-capable access models are feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google as trust anchors. Keep the demonstrator scope lean and defensible; do not expand into feature-rich product development. Phase two funding is conditional on phase one producing a published feasibility report, at least one formal institutional engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, or a related public-sector stakeholder, and a documented financing plan covering the remainder of the project.

Phase three (months 17–24) focuses on procurement, standards, and continuity intervention around AltID and related digital identity planning. Using the feasibility report, demonstrators, and coalition built in earlier phases, the project seeks inclusion of requirements stating that Danish digital identity systems should not depend exclusively on Apple or Google app stores, platform integrity APIs, or proprietary push-notification infrastructure, and should include a platform-neutral or otherwise certified fallback access path for critical public authentication. The project also engages with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework, with the aim of aligning Danish advocacy with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in. The project's role is not to dictate architecture directly, but to create technical evidence, procurement language, and standards pressure that make supplier-neutral and fallback-capable design harder to exclude. If formal traction with AltID is weak by month 16, phase three should de-scope toward publication, coalition consolidation, and EU-facing standards input rather than pretending Danish procurement influence has been secured.

The project team should include a lead researcher in mobile security and authentication protocols, a regulatory and compliance specialist familiar with Danish and EU digital identity frameworks (including eIDAS and NSIS), a policy and public-affairs coordinator experienced in Danish digital governance and procurement processes, a technical lead for the demonstrator work, and at least one additional engineer or contractor covering web authentication, hardware-token workflows, or Linux/GrapheneOS-related implementation. The technical staffing should avoid dependence on a single specialist. Assume the project's primary physical base is in Copenhagen, Denmark, at a Danish university, independent research institution, or security-qualified R&D facility providing access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings. Budget for institutional overhead, external security review, and limited legal/procurement support explicitly rather than treating them as absorbed costs.

Success criteria: by month 24, the project has produced a published feasibility and risk report on platform-neutral and fallback-capable Danish digital identity; delivered two working non-production demonstrators (one mobile-oriented, one browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback); secured a formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or another relevant authority to the project's proposal; and achieved inclusion of platform-neutrality, fallback access, continuity, resilience, or supplier-concentration language in at least one official AltID-related design, policy, consultation, or procurement document. A stretch goal is that the project's EU-level engagement contributes to language in relevant European Digital Identity Wallet materials that supports platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms. Do not use consumer adoption rates as a primary KPI for this project, because it is not shipping a production identity product.

Pick a realistic scenario that accounts for institutional inertia, regulatory friction, incumbent resistance, financing uncertainty, and the likelihood that convenience arguments will be used against platform-neutral alternatives. Do not assume constructive cooperation from existing operators. The primary lever is not technical disruption for its own sake, but the combination of resilience framing, procurement intervention, standards pressure, and credible technical demonstration.
